name: Wheat
description: Staple cereal; mainly rain-fed in highlands. Largely imported to meet national demand but still widely cultivated where rainfall allows.

name: Barley
description: Used for animal feed and some human consumption; well adapted to drier, rain-fed conditions.

name: Olive (Olea europaea)
regions: Highlands, terraces across northern & central Jordan
description: About 11 million olive trees in Jordan; covers ~72% of fruit tree area, ~30% of cultivated area; production ~180,000–200,000 tons/year. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1} Also, ~80% of olive production is turned into oil. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

name: Grapes (Vitis vinifera)
description: Table grapes, raisin and wine varieties are grown in both irrigated and rainfed terraces historically and currently.

name: Tomato
description: High-value irrigated crop and major export product. One of the main vegetables driving horticultural growth in Jordan.

name: Cucumber
description: Frequently grown in greenhouses and irrigated fields; high domestic self-sufficiency in vegetables supports this prevalence.

name: Potato
description: Important staple vegetable with both domestic consumption and local markets.

name: Onion
regions: Various agricultural zones, irrigated & rain-fed patches
description: High production within Jordan; among vegetables where Jordan achieves self-sufficiency ratios above 100%.

name: Eggplant
description: Part of the core vegetable basket in Jordan; grown under irrigation where possible for stable yields.

name: Citrus (Oranges, lemons, etc.)
description: Important irrigated fruit crop; climate and water availability limit its range mostly to valley and lower elevations.

name: Date Palm
regions: Aqaba region, select Jordan Valley farms
description: Cultivated in warm, low-elevation zones. Not as extensive as in Gulf nations, but present in specialized areas.

name: Pomegranate
regions: Koura district, Wadi Zeqlab, northern/central perennial-stream areas
description: Not a national crop but regionally important; processing/export potential increasing.
sources: News article: Pomegranate harvest in Koura district

name: Saffron (Crocus sativus)
regions: Karak highlands and experimental farms (600–1400 m)
description: Small niche sector, often on pilot farms. Cultivation is limited but valued for its high price and low volume.

name: Almond
description: Orchards exist but are a minor crop; often intercropped or in mixed dryland farming systems.

name: Fig
description: Traditional fruit cultivated in small orchards and household gardens; resilient in drier zones.

name: Lentil
description: Grows in rotation with cereals; contributes to soil nitrogen and local consumption.
sources: Major field crops list in Jordan study

name: Chickpea
description: Widely grown pulse; integral to local diets and cropping rotations.

name: Alfalfa (Medicago sativa)
description: High water demand; used for livestock feed and trade; among the top crops by irrigation requirement in Jordan studies.
sources: Major field crops list Jordan study

name: Retama (Retama raetam)
description: Native shrub of the Jordanian desert; part of rangeland flora and grazing ecology.
sources: Plants of Jordan checklist (Jordan Plant Red List Volumes) :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

name: Ziziphus (Sidr / Nabuk)
description: Native fruit-bearing shrub/tree with cultural and medicinal uses; appears in floristic surveys of Jordan.
sources: Plants of Jordan checklist (Jordan Plant Red List) :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

name: Oak (Quercus calliprinos)
description: Native oak forming cooler, wetter zone woodlands in northern/western highlands; important for biodiversity and protection against erosion.
sources: Plants of Jordan checklist (Jordan Plant Red List) :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}

name: Iris nigricans (Black Iris)
description: National flower of Jordan. Endangered species. Endemic to Jordan. Flourishes in specific habitat with threatened conservation status. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

name: Rheum palaestinum (Desert Rhubarb)
description: Unique plant adapted for self-watering via leaves that channel condensation. Grows on cliffs amongst desert rocks. Not a cultivated crop, but ecologically interesting and culturally notable. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

name: Iris petrana (Petra Iris)
regions: Southern Jordan (around Tafilah), desert plains, eastern side of Wadi Araba
description: Spring blooming wild iris; requires well-drained sandy or loess soil; used in conservation programs. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

name: Echium judaeum (Judean viper's bugloss)
regions: Southern Syria, southern Lebanon, Israel, Jordan – southern parts
description: Annual wild flowering plant in the Boraginaceae family; flowers bloom Feb-May. Not cultivated but part of wild flora. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

name: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum)
regions: Herbal gardens, wild/herbal plant zones across Jordan
description: Used as medicinal/herbal plant and spice; multiple projects highlight it among herbs feasible for cultivation. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

name: Origanum jordanicum
regions: Mountainous and rocky areas, north/central Jordan
description: Wild medicinal aromatic plant. Part of the list of endemic herbs in Jordan. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

name: Cactaceae / Succulents / Endemic shrubs
regions: Desert zones, Araba, Jordan Valley fringes
description: Various succulents and wild shrubs; not all well studied; many are threatened by overgrazing / habitat loss. (Example lists include plants in Med-O-Med program) :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

name: Pistacia palaestina (Wild Pistache)
regions: Northern / Western highlands, Mediterranean zones in Jordan
description: Wild tree species part of oak/pistache woodlands; valued for shade and locally for wild fruit and wood. Recorded in Royal Botanic Garden surveys. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}

name: Salvia spp. (Sage)
regions: Herbal zones, wild areas, sometimes cultivated gardens
description: Salvia and other herbs such as rosemary, oregano, mint identified in programs enhancing herbs cultivation. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

name: Achillea santolina (Santolina, wild yarrow)
regions: Arid & semi-arid zones, Badia, Jordan Valley edges
description: Used in traditional medicine and as insect repellent; frequent in wild flora surveys. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

name: Thymus bovei (Wild thyme / Zaatar barrie)
regions: Northern & central hilly zones, rocky slopes
description: Highly used in folk medicine for digestive and respiratory ailments; identified in Badia edible‐wild plant studies. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

name: Malva sylvestris (Common mallow)
regions: Badia, Northern Badia, field margins, wet spots
description: Leaves & flowers edible; used traditionally in foods and remedies; edible wild plant studies report high uptake among local communities. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

name: Scorzonera schweinfurthii
description: Consumed wild by communities in Northern Badia; significant in edible wild‐plant uses; edible parts used raw or cooked. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

name: Artemisia herba-alba (Shieh)
description: Used by many for gastrointestinal, respiratory ailments; strong cultural usage among Bedouin populations. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

name: Lavandula spp. (Lavender)
regions: Mountainous & hilly zones, gardens, wild areas
description: Used in perfumes and essential oils; project to enhance cultivation due to demand. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

name: Citrullus colocynthis (Bitter apple / Handhal)
description: Used medicinally, though toxic; parts are used in folk herbalism, but with caution. Identified in Badia surveys. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

name: Peganum harmala (Syrian rue)
regions: Desert plains, Badia, edges of cultivation
description: Used in traditional medicine, internal/external applications; somewhat less common due to habitat disturbance. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}

name: Adiantum capillus-veneris (Maidenhair fern)
regions: Shady moist ravines, springs, highland zones
description: Less widespread; often restricted to microhabitats with moisture; reported in some herbal/medicinal surveys. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

name: Wild strawberry (Fragaria spp.)
regions: High elevations, forest edges, moist zones
description: Not major commercial crop, but used locally by foragers or small households for fruit; less documented than herbs. (Mention in flora checklists) :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}

name: Cupressus sempervirens (Mediterranean cypress)
regions: Old forest groves, highland zones, some isolated specimens in cultivated landscapes
description: Some ancient trees remain, highly valued; fewer in number due to land use change. :contentReference[oaicite:10]{index=10}

name: Teucrium polium (Jadah)
description: Widely used for folk medicine (digestive, antispasmodic). One of the frequently consumed wild edible/medicinal herbs in Northern Badia. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

name: Artemisia herba-alba (Shieh)
description: Used extensively by local communities for gastrointestinal problems, respiratory ailments. Very common medicinal wild species. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

name: Anthemis cotula (Mayweed / Beboneg)
description: Used as medicinal and aromatic; part of the non-wood forest products list. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

name: Capparis spinosa (Caper bush / Kubbar)
description: Fruit & buds used in local cuisine; also aromatic uses. Listed among major aromatic/wild edible plants. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

name: Laurus nobilis (Bay laurel / Ghar)
description: Leaves used as spice, perfume, traditional medicine; found wild and sometimes cultivated. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

name: Eruca sativa (Rocket / Arugula)
regions: Field margins, wild herb zones, sometimes cultivated
description: Used both wild and cultivated; seeds and leaves edible; included in aromatic / edible plant studies. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

name: Anchusa strigosa
regions: Eastern Mediterranean, rocky slopes, woodland edges
description: Found in Jordan; part of pollen/aromatic/floral wild species. Not heavily used in cuisine but valued for medicinal / ornamental uses. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

name: Iris nigricans (Black Iris)
description: National flower of Jordan. Endangered wild species. Requires conservation. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}

name: Podonosma orientalis (Golden Drop)
regions: Wadi Zerka Main (Moab), Petra limestone cliffs, desert steppes
description: Used for cleaning (soap substitute because of saponin), traditional remedies, and edible flowers. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

name: Salvia hierosolymitana (Jerusalem Sage)
regions: Rocky fields, open ground in Mediterranean-type zones
description: Wild perennial used in ornamental and possibly medicinal uses. Not a major food plant but valued in folk/traditional medicine. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}

name: Capparis spinosa (Caper bush)
description: Flower buds and fruits pickled/used in local cuisine; tolerant of drought and saline soils.
sources: Plants of Jordan / FAO / local ethnobotany

name: Laurus nobilis (Bay laurel)
description: Leaves used as spice and medicinally; found wild and occasionally cultivated.

name: Capparis ovata / Capparis aegyptia (Local caper relatives)
description: Related species to the caper bush, used similarly in local diets.

name: Coriandrum sativum (Coriander) — wild/cultivated
description: Both cultivated and sometimes volunteer/wild in fields; leaves and seeds used in cooking.
sources: Local agriculture guides / herb cultivation projects

name: Foeniculum vulgare (Fennel)
description: Leaves and seeds used as spice and medicine; grows wild and cultivated.

name: Nigella sativa (Black cumin)
description: Seed used as spice and traditional medicine; niche cultivation exists.

name: Salvia rosmarinus / Rosmarinus officinalis (Rosemary)
description: Widely used for culinary and aromatic oil production; grows wild on rocky slopes.

name: Mentha spp. (Mint)
description: Many species/varieties used for tea and flavoring; often cultivated near homes.

name: Matricaria aurea / Chamaemelum nobile (Chamomile)
regions: Open fields, field margins, Badia seasonal areas
description: Used for herbal tea and remedies; some local cultivation for sale.

name: Capparis (capparis spp. variations)
description: Multiple Capparis species occur; local economic use includes pickled buds and conservation interest.
sources: Plants of Jordan / local food reports

name: Aconitum spp. (monkshood) — cautionary wild herbs
description: Some wild alpine herbs are toxic; reported in floristic checklists—important to identify before use.

name: Plantago ovata (Psyllium, Isabgol)
description: Seed husks used as fiber laxative; cultivated in small scale in some regions.

name: Capparis spinosa variant (rock caper)
description: Closely related caper variants used locally; resilient to drought.

name: Rumex vesicarius (Bladder dock)
description: Young leaves sometimes used cooked as a leafy vegetable in rural diets.

name: Glaucium arabicum (Horn poppy relative)
description: Part of wild flora surveys; cultural and ecological interest.

name: Paronychia argentea
description: Used locally in folk medicine and as groundcover; appears in edible wild plant literature.

name: Plantago syriaca
description: Traditional medicinal uses; noted in Jordan floristic lists.

name: Anagallis arvensis (Scarlet pimpernel)
description: Common field weed with occasional traditional uses; widespread in disturbed soils.

name: Capparis cultivated (local varieties)
description: Some farmers cultivate capers commercially for local and export markets.

name: Cousinia dayi
description: One of several narrowly distributed taxa recorded in Jordan’s endemic lists.
sources: Plants of Jordan / endemic plant lists

name: Rumex coccineus (Red dock)
description: Occasional edible use where available; part of localized spring flora.

name: Pyrus syriaca (Wild pear)
description: Wild fruit tree used by foragers and sometimes protected on terraces.
sources: Royal Botanic Garden / Plants of Jordan

name: Capparis offshoot (market supply)
description: Small value chain exists for pickled capers sold domestically and regionally.

name: Tamarix spp. (Tamarisk)
description: Often colonizes saline or brackish sites; important for erosion control in some valleys.

name: Morina longifolia
description: Attractive perennial of montane habitats; noted in specialized floras.

name: Dianthus spp. (Carnations / wild pinks)
description: Numerous Dianthus species occur regionally; some have local medicinal or ornamental uses.

name: Aconitum / rare alpine herbs group
description: Small populations of rare alpine herbs appear in montane checklists; conservation interest.

name: Iris vartanii
description: One of the Oncocyclus irises native to Jordan; rare in wild. Occurs in desert sand and loess plains. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
sources: Iris nigricans wiki (lists Iris vartanii among Jordan’s irises) :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

name: Iris atrofusca
description: Black-flowered iris, very localized. Occurs in low rainfall, sandy / loess soil. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

name: Desert Rhubarb (Rheum palaestinum)
description: Has specialized leaf morphology that funnels dew / vapor towards roots; adapted to extreme dryness. Protected in some areas. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

name: Quercus ithaburensis (Mount Tabor Oak)
description: Important oak species in Jordan’s woodlands; more resilient; national tree. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

name: Lotus jordanii
description: Listed among plants native to Jordan; wild flowering legume. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

name: Cousinia dayia
regions: Specific endemic localities per Jordan / Lebanon region
description: Listed in Med-O-Med program as characteristic endemic plant. Habitat often rocky limestone areas. :contentReference[oaicite:10]{index=10}

name: Scrophularia nabataeorum
description: Listed as endemic in Med-O-Med species lists; limited distribution. :contentReference[oaicite:12]{index=12}

name: Silene hussonii
description: Listed among rare or endangered flora in Jordan; occurs in specific microhabitats. :contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}
sources: Conservation and Sustainable Use… Jordan flora rare species list :contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}

name: Colchicum tunicatum
description: One of the rare/endemic species in Jordan plant conservation reports. :contentReference[oaicite:16]{index=16}

name: Aegilops bicornis
description: In Med-O-Med list of phylogenetic resources; wild relative of wheat/barley; important for genetic diversity. :contentReference[oaicite:18]{index=18}

name: Centaurea procerrens
description: Part of rare flora lists; conservation interest. :contentReference[oaicite:20]{index=20}

name: Lathyrus gloeospermus
description: Listed as endemic or regionally rare in Jordan conservation literature. :contentReference[oaicite:22]{index=22}

name: Chickpea
description: Chickpeas are a staple legume in Jordanian agriculture, grown primarily in rain-fed areas. Production has varied over the years, with significant fluctuations due to climatic conditions. In 2016, production reached approximately 2,022 metric tons.

name: Clover
description: Clover is widely cultivated for animal fodder. In 2016, production peaked at about 337,290 metric tons, reflecting its importance in livestock feeding.

name: Garlic
description: Garlic is a significant crop in Jordan, with production reaching approximately 2,267 metric tons in 2016. Supports both domestic consumption and export markets.

name: Maize
description: Maize is an essential cereal crop in Jordan, with production reaching about 29,879 metric tons in 2016. Serves as a vital food source and animal feed.

name: Sesame
description: Sesame production in Jordan has seen fluctuations, with a peak of approximately 40.7 metric tons in 2015. Valued for its oil-rich seeds.

name: Sorghum
description: Sorghum is a drought-tolerant cereal crop, with production reaching about 189.5 metric tons in 2016. Primarily used for animal feed and some traditional dishes.
